I think it would be useful to massage the injection flow; for example, if I say "we need to use line separators on the catalog we provide (if they are already there we need to render new lines on the tail tui)" when claude code first starts it might not know what "the catalog" means or what the "tail tui" is. perhaps we can use some of the existing docs to not only find information relevant to the ask but also information about what the nouns or things being mentioned (clearly with an expectation that the model will know what I'm talking about is) Thoughts?

The instinct is right but the wiki has no entity definitions, only principle/decision guides — so even perfect noun-grounding retrieval has nothing to select. Suggested dual-bar selection (task bar + grounding bar) and adding entity guides to the corpus.

Ran verbose; the real problem isn't content or retrieval — the gate model (deepseek-v4-flash) with rig's default think=false snap-bails EVERYTHING as NOTHING_RELEVANT. glm-5.1:cloud with think=false picks correctly in 2.8s.
glm-5.1:cloud solves the bail — correct picks, 2.8s gate, full pipeline compiles with accurate citations. Config now live on glm-5.1:cloud for both stages.
what is "compile"? I'm confused about what this roles are tbh

SELECT picks which files (titles+summaries only), COMPILE picks which lines within those files (returns line ranges), then Rust slices the verbatim text. COMPILE doesn't write anything — it's a pointer, not a synthesizer. The name is misleading.
I think we need to take it back to the compile where it synthesis the relevant stuff -- only change is that it MUST include sources for all its claims with file:line#

Implemented synthesis-with-mandatory-citations compile, removed Hits: section, deleted old line-range machinery. Validated end-to-end: dense briefing, 6 accurate citations, ~1.7KB vs old ~19KB. Three wiki guides are now stale contradicting the new design.

Consolidated to one canonical guide (inject-is-librarian-not-answerer), deleted two duplicates, pruned inbound links. Flagged that capture accretes rather than updates — root cause is the pipeline, not a one-time mess.

Explained the full capture flow: triage gate, agent loop with wiki_list/wiki_read, and mutation tools. The accretion gap is that the prompt never tells the model to prefer revise/remove over add/create, and the only dedup guard is exact-slug.
Prompt fix now (three rules: one topic=one guide, reconcile don't accrete, scope the recall bias), defer the action-enum structural redesign as follow-up.

Full session recap: started with inject noun-grounding, found think=false gate bug (fixed with glm), redesigned compile to synthesis+citations, reconciled wiki guides, diagnosed capture accretion, and was about to apply a 3-rule prompt fix pending git safety check.

the prompt solution is too hopeful; the tool solution might not be enough -- let's think furhter -- what would the flow be of the capture path? what other alternatives can we think of? let's think outside the box a bit

Proposed 6 alternatives: mechanical routing, capture-as-diff, statement-level upsert, guide-centric loop, deferred compaction, and event-sourced claims. Recommended mechanical routing + action enum as highest-leverage now, capture-as-diff as clean long-term, and compaction as insurance.

how about we force it go through a set of stages? 0. inject in the system prompt all the guides that exist (title + summary + last-modified) 1. force the model to analyze the transcript for all the explicitly stated claims, with extreme care for every claim done by the user (99% of the time we want to keep those, unless they are mechanical orders (e.g. "commit this work") and with implicit agreement for what the agent said/responded. -- We should probably differentiate user claims such that they are ALWAYS stored, even when the user changed their mind, that's still relevant! An agent hallucination being fixed is not relevant. 2. Give a tool to the agent persist_claims([]) -> all it does is add all user claims in some append-only log -- durable --> this tool uses a model itself, in parallel to find guides that are good candidates (not using the RAG, rather giving it a list of all the guides with the title, summary and first 500 lines of each guide and all the claims the agent wants to persist. The agent just gets back a list of possible guides that might need to be updated. 3. The agent then iterates over each claim in parallel, using file reading tools and whatever else. I don't know, I'm not sure if this is the way -- I'm just thinking. Run three opus agents to brainstorm.

Three opus agents converged: parallel writes are wrong (wiki serializes on lock), don't add a second log (upgrade citations.log), full-wiki broadcast doesn't scale. Converged on Extract→Authority Gate→Route→Reconcile→History pipeline with mechanical authority attribution (deriving author from transcript turn, not classification).
I'm unclear of what's the high-level of the destination you're proposing -- how would it work?

The destination: guides become read-only projections of an append-only claim log. The model never edits a guide — it only appends claim events with supersede links. Projection rebuilds guides from live claims, making accretion structurally impossible. User mind-changes are preserved as archaeology breadcrumbs.
when you say "read" you mean, during the inject phase?
No — "read" means rendering/projecting the guide from the claim log (capture-side, off the hot path). Inject always reads the materialized .md file, unchanged.

superseeded notes that come from the agent can just be dropped from the source of truth once they become superseeded -- supersseded notes from the user should continue to carry throughout the guide -- if something used to work as A and it started to work as B it's ok, even desirable, for the guide to include a nudge to how it used to work (imagine if the refactor of the code didn't completely catch everything and now we have seemingly slightly weird code, but the context of how it used to work before would explain it and provide context (exactly what we want this project to do!) on how things had been implemented in some way

Spin 1 complete with caveat: 0 duplication, hallucinations excluded, but the substring scorer overcounts accretion. Real weakness: superseding a rule doesn't cascade to its dependent definitions. Spin 3 reads best but silently drops facts. Spin 2 still running (slowest). Recommends Spin 1.5 hybrid: write-time bookkeeping + LLM projection-with-history.
fix the scorer to use the LLM judge instead of substring matching

Spin 3 in: 0 accretion/duplication, highest readability, but silently drops facts (4/6 coverage) and has flat history with no provenance. Spin 2 is elegant but worst in practice (didn't finish, most expensive). Verdict: Spin 3 out for knowledge base, Spin 2 deprioritized, Spin 1 wins empirically.
so spin 2 rewrite all guides every single time capture runs?

Only touched guides, but each re-projects the full claim history — cost grows unboundedly. Pure Spin 2's elegance erodes into Spin 1's bookkeeping once you prune for cost, which is most of what Spin 1 already does.

not really; what an agent said/infered IS part of what is captured -- it's just subject to be removed when a correction lands later, but it's ok, even better if the wiki distinguishes what are explicit directions vs implicit directions for the product ("add oauth integration" -> explicit direction "I will add google oauth support too" -> implicit until the user ackwnoledges -- if later the user says "only support github oauth" then the google oauth support product direction (agent-generated) gets deleted and the explicit "only github oauth" gets codified as made permanent)

Implemented tag-don't-drop: agent claims tagged as implicit metadata (never rendered), never auto-deleted. +37% claim density recovered, but 81% of claims came out provisional — the mechanical definition of "implicit" is too broad (agent-narrated facts ≠ proposals). Three options: classify fact vs proposal, quiet the marker, or reconsider if the distinction earns its keep.
tell me more about what did you observe that got us to this decision point -- like, what's the reason you are popping this question?

81% of claims tagged provisional because author-by-turn marks all agent narration (including settled facts) as "implicit" — the proxy (who typed it) doesn't match the concept (directive vs proposal). A label on 81% of statements carries zero information and actively mislabels core facts as tentative.

Launched comparison agent. Current live wiki: 159 guides (accreted). Gold standard: 27. Live wiki has massive duplication and fragmentation vs gold's coherent structure.
tell me more about the embedding router

Embedding router = retrieve-then-rerank for ROUTE. Embed each claim, cosine-retrieve top-K similar existing guides, then cheap LLM picks among those candidates (or NEW). Breaks the split-vs-merge tension because the similarity signal is structural, not prompt-bias. Reuses existing embedding infra (MiniLM-384, vec_chunks). Main engineering cost: embed-on-write freshness + top-K tuning.
Building embedding router and pushing. Also: do I have a good idea of what made it create so many guides?
do you have a good idea of what happened that made it create so many?

Yes — ROUTE altitude is tuned to "one mechanism per guide" which for a large project produces 172 thin guides (45 citing a single session). The prompt contradicts itself (says both "one mechanism" and "~25-30 guides"). Per-session capture has no global view, so it can't know that avatar-fade-in should fold into a broader topic.

I checked the docs/wiki of nostr-multi-platform and its pretty much a disaster -- is that the right place I'm supposed to look at?

Yes, that's the archeologist's output. The disaster has three causes: over-granularity (172 guides, 45 citing a single session), unresolved citations ([^id] markers with no definitions), and empty See Also sections. The content is real but the presentation is broken and fragmented.

Not all justified — 45 of 172 cite a single session. ~5 nip-* and ~11 relay-* guides are genuinely separate; the rest are over-split (avatar×3, profile×2, etc.). The real fix options: (a) group into topics/ hierarchy, (b) merge near-dups, or (c) both. Recommended (a)/(c): the 172 aren't redundant, they're unorganized.
is this really what the archeologist came up with?

Yes, verbatim. Showed a complete example guide: one session, one trivial fact ("avatars fade in over 0.2s"), its own page, dangling citation, empty See Also. The pipeline produces this by design — ROUTE is tuned to sub-concern altitude, citations never get definitions, and See Also is appended empty.
kill it and let's fix the pipeline first

Killed podcast-player backfill. Built wiki-tidy (strips dangling citations to HTML comments, removes empty See Also — fixes 170/172 guides dry-run) and wiki-doctor (--detect-only at tau 0.55 clusters 172 guides into 8 coherent topic groups). The clusters are better for grouping than merging — recommended organizing into topics/ hierarchy rather than collapsing.
besides the after-the-fact fix -- are we fixing the capture path so that it uses topics and doesn't create this big mess?

No — the capture path isn't fixed yet. wiki-tidy/doctor are after-the-fact cleanup. The real fixes: (1) ROUTE altitude prompt deleted the "~25-30 guides" nonsense (shipped), (2) topic routing needs full-catalog RERANK + topic metadata (designed, not built — blast radius is real, needs validation), (3) staleness/retirement is a genuine gap (designed, not built). Recommended building topic routing + validating before trusting it.

it's impossible to estimate what a healthy project's wiki in how many guides it has? that's nonsense! the prompt shouldn't say that at all.

yes, a) route should output a topic -- and route should have in its context a full view of everything that currently exists and what each entry is about (i.e. title and summary).

Deleted the count target from ROUTE prompt (committed). Designed but didn't build: full-catalog topic routing (RERANK sees all guides by topic, emits constrained topic metadata — recommends flat files + topic metadata over physical folders), and staleness retirement (demote + breadcrumb, never delete; code-grounding strongest signal but version-conflict more precise for v1). Wrote comprehensive plan to docs/product-spec/topic-routing-and-staleness-plan.md.

Phase 1 (altitude validation rebuild) launched: fresh --output-dir rebuild of nostr with the new altitude prompt, ~1.5h run. Three decisions needed for Phase 2/3: D1 topic metadata vs folders, D2 re-capture vs retopic, D3 staleness scope.

Definitive: topic ratio pinned at exactly 1.00 across two checkpoints (33/33 then 48/48). Zero topics ever held more than one guide. Stopping the loop — the validation question is answered. Topic routing needs the embedding-router treatment, or wiki-doctor's clustering can group post-hoc.

Found and fixed the root cause of validation failures: 120s hard-coded HTTP timeout in call_model_blocking killed the doctor's batch taxonomy calls. Added call_model_blocking_with_timeout (600s for batch, 120s default for hot path). Relaunched retopic on TENEX-TUI wiki.

Loop complete: pc wiki doctor --retopic works. 74 guides → 7 coherent topics (10.57 guides/topic). LLM taxonomy groups semantically (nostr-protocol cluster groups kind1+negentropy+relay-pin+event-kind-registry — guides sharing no vocabulary). Committed to master (eab6d2a): --retopic flag, 600s batch timeout, 77 tests pass.

Applied --retopic to TENEX-TUI wiki. Caught and fixed a data-loss bug (strip_empty_see_also deleted cited content under See Also). Reverted, fixed, re-applied cleanly. 74 guides → 10 coherent topics, git-verified lossless. Committed (709317d9). Remaining: apply --retopic to other wikis (nostr needs a context-fitting model), and Phase 3 staleness.

Phase 3 staleness retirement: lead v1 with version-conflict detection (within-topic version string comparison — most precise, near-zero false positives, nails the nmp 0.1.0→0.2.0 case). Dead-file-reference as flag-only (too many renames/ADRs for auto-demote). LLM confirm forces 3-way classification: CURRENT / HISTORICAL (intentionally about past — leave alone) / STALE (demote). Demote = status:superseded frontmatter + banner with forward pointer. Dry-run default, reversible. Defer cascade and auto-revive. Three decisions needed: D1 signals scope, D2 auto-revive in v1 or defer, D3 demote with or without forward pointer.
